coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen spillover effect enhanced by carbon quantum dots activated MoS2
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Lili
Zhang, Xiaoyun
Zhu, Shifan
Xu, Yixue
Wang, Yuqiao
coredata.dc:description: The weak hydrogen adsorption and slow catalytic kinetics are unfavorable for MoS2 as a catalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in alkaline solution. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high proton adsorption energy were expected to enhance the proton adsorption of MoS2 during the HER process. The CQDs activated MoS2 can establish a suitable hydrogen adsorption gradient due to the surface charge redistribution to promote the hydrogen spillover pathway. After the CQDs were loaded on MoS2, the charge distribution would gradually transfer from the C sites of CQDs to the S sites on MoS2 surface, showing a charge gradient distribution calculated by density functional theory predictions. The adsorbed hydrogen can spill over from hydrogen-enriched CQDs to hydrogen-deficient MoS2 in HER. The process could be monitored by the quasi in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The enhanced hydrogen spillover effect by CQDs activated MoS2 can pave the way for the design and preparation of efficient and low-cost catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
MoS2
Surface charge redistribution
Hydrogen spillover
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs with different diameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the efficient HER on the surface of MoS2@modifier catalysts. Calculated proton adsorption energies: (b) C4, C5, C6, C7, C8, C9 and C10 clusters and (c) C20, C30, C40 and C50 clusters. (d) The proton adsorption energies of C6 clusters with different amounts.

MoS2@CQDs: (a) Isosurface of differential charge density (0.002 e/Bohr3), 2D slices of (b) charge density distribution and (c) ELF mapping. d) 3D configuration of ELF (ELF = 0.85). (e) Calculated LDOS and (f) PDOS (Yellow: Sulfur atoms, Purple: Molybdenum atoms, Gray: Carbon atoms, Red: Oxygen atoms).
(a) Calculated ΔGH* diagram for hydrogen spillover on MoS2@CQDs. (b) -COHP plots of the C/S–H bonding at the corresponding adsorption site. (c) Illustration of the correlation between ICOHP and ΔEH*.

(a) Schematic diagram of the transport properties in MoS2@MCQDs. (b) HRTEM image of MoS2@MCQDs. (c) SAED pattern of the corresponding MoS2 region. (d) FFT patterns. CQDs: (e) Lattice fringe and (f) diameter distribution. (g) Elemental mappings of Mo, S, C, and O elements from MoS2@MCQDs.

(a) HER polarization curves. (b) Tafel plots. (c) Compared ECSA values. (d) C φ
vs. ƞ plots of MoS2, MoS2@LCQDs, MoS2@MCQDs and MoS2@SCQDs. (e) Current density vs. working time at a constant overpotential of 150 mV.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen spillover effect enhanced by carbon quantum dots activated MoS2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Photoelectrochemistry and Devices, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Photoelectrochemistry and Devices
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Photoelectrochemistry and Devices, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The weak hydrogen adsorption and slow catalytic kinetics are unfavorable for MoS2 as a catalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in alkaline solution. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high proton adsorption energy were expected to enhance the proton adsorption of MoS2 during the HER process. The CQDs activated MoS2 can establish a suitable hydrogen adsorption gradient due to the surface charge redistribution to promote the hydrogen spillover pathway. After the CQDs were loaded on MoS2, the charge distribution would gradually transfer from the C sites of CQDs to the S sites on MoS2 surface, showing a charge gradient distribution calculated by density functional theory predictions. The adsorbed hydrogen can spill over from hydrogen-enriched CQDs to hydrogen-deficient MoS2 in HER. The process could be monitored by the quasi in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The enhanced hydrogen spillover effect by CQDs activated MoS2 can pave the way for the design and preparation of efficient and low-cost catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is a sustainable method for hydrogen generation to realize the hydrogen economy [1–3]. Nowadays, the commercial Pt-based catalysts are used to overcome the energy barrier of water splitting. The development of non-precious metal catalysts is one of the research hotspots to replace the scarce platinum-based catalysts. Two-dimensional molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a candidate for HER due to its unique properties of structure and chemistry. The weak hydrogen adsorption and slow kinetics hinder the activities of MoS2-based catalysts owing to the unsuitable surface charge states for HER [4]. The continuous efforts have been made to solve the problem such as morphology regulation [5], defect introduction [6–8], phase tuning [9] and heterostructure engineering [10,11]. The above all are commonly used to modulate the surface structure and composition of MoS2 to enhance HER activity. Carbon-based materials have been widely used to enhance the catalytic activity of transition metal compounds [12,13]. Especially, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were concerned due to the large specific surface energy, good electrical conductivity and environmentally friendly in the field of photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [14,15]. However, it has always been a hotpot topic on how to enhance the role of CQDs in the HER catalytic process and the related reaction mechanism.

The hydrogen spillover pathway has been developed to activate the HER activity of scarce metal-based binary catalysts [16–18]. This pathway was considered from the adsorption sites of the noble metal to the desorption sites on the support to guarantee the hydrogen generation. For example, the Ru1Fe1/CoP catalyst was designed to facilitate the hydrogen spillover by using the work function difference between metals and supports [19]. The multicomponent interface was usually the weak link for electron transfer, which would be unfavorable for hydrogen spillover from catalyst surface [20]. Therefore, the development of hydrogen spillover effect at the atomic and molecular scale is of great significance to promote the catalytic hydrogen evolution of multicomponent non-precious metals.

In this work, we reported a method to visually describe the electron distribution on the surface of multicomponent and multidimensional catalysts using density functional theory (DFT), helping to design and prepare the MoS2@CQDs catalysts for the effective hydrogen spillover pathway. The surface charge distribution and electronic structure change were investigated by the differential charge density, electron localization function (ELF) and density of states (DOS). The transport pathway of adsorbed hydrogen (H*) can be established by the Gibbs free energy change of hydrogen (ΔGH*) in various active sites to evaluate the effect of CQDs using DFT predictions. The MoS2@CQDs with hydrogen spillover pathway would be designed and developed as advanced HER catalysts by the combination of theoretical calculation and experimental verification.

Carbon clusters were constructed through various carbon atom numbers defined as C4, C5, C6, C7, C8, C9, C10, C20, C30, C40 and C50 clusters. The favorite CQDs was simplified to C6 cluster model owing to the limitation of computational resource and efficiency. The primary 2H–MoS2 model with P63/mmc was achieved from Materials Project. The lattice constant was α = β = 90°, γ = 120°, a = b = 3.18 Å, c = 13.98 Å. Mo atom connected to the 6 S atoms in 2H–MoS2. 3 S atoms formed a triangle with the same direction on the upper and lower surfaces. The primary MoS2 slab model consisted of 16 Mo and 32 S atoms based on 2H–MoS2 (002) plane. A vacuum of 16 Å was used to avoid spurious interactions in non-periodic direction (the z direction). The MoS2@CQDs model would be composed of 2H–MoS2 (002) plane (4 × 4 × 1) and C6 cluster by Mo–O–C bond.

The Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) were carried out in DFT calculations by using the plane-wave pseudopotential method [21]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was used in the scheme of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) to describe the exchange-correlation functional [22]. All the calculations were performed using the framework of spin-polarized and geometry optimization, and set at the energy cutoff of 400 eV and Monkhorst-Pack k-points of 2 × 2 × 1. The geometry relaxation was converged to 0.02 eV/Å and 1.0 × 10−5 eV/atom. After geometry relaxation the electronic structure and the system energy were calculated. The crystal orbital Hamilton population (COHP) and integrated COHP (ICOHP) have been carried out on Lobster 3.0 codes to estimate the bonding strength between H* and carbon/sulfur atoms. The proton adsorption energy (Ea) and ΔGH* were used to estimate HER performance. The calculation formulas were following as:
(1)
E a
=
E
(
C n
+ m H )
−
E
(
C n
)
−

×
E H
(2)
Δ
G
H ∗
= Δ E + Δ Z P E − T Δ S
where
E
(
C n
+ m H )
,
E
(
C n
)
and
E
H 2
are the energies of various carbon clusters with different number of protons, various carbon clusters and protons. The n and m are the number of carbon atoms and adsorbed protons, respectively. EH can be obtained by
E H
=
1 2
E
H 2
.
Δ
E
Z P E
is the zero-point correction.
Δ S
is the entropy difference between the adsorbed state and the gas phase and the entropy correction is from the gas phase of H2
( Δ
S
H
≈
− 1 / 2
S
H 2
)
[23,24].

The AIMD simulations were carried out at NVT ensemble (T = 300 K) using VASP software, with the same level of the geometry relaxation. A time step of 1 femto-second (fs) and energy cutoff of 400 eV were used to integrate the equations of motion. The temperature was maintained at ∼300 K using a Nose–Hoover thermostat. The 10 ps trajectory was generated and used for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs with different diameters
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were prepared by the electro-oxidation method using the direct current of 15 V and two Pt electrodes in the electrolyte of NaOH (1 g), ethanol (140 mL) and distilled water (10 mL) [25]. After 24 h of electrolysis, the yellow-brown carbon dots/ethanol solution was obtained by standing for 3 days. The different diameters of CQDs (≤10 nm) were separated by the various centrifugal speeds of 3000 r/min, 6000 r/min and 10000 r/min, respectively, referring to large CQDs (LCQDs), moderate CQDs (MCQDs) and small CQDs (SCQDs).

The CQDs solution (1 mg/mL, 5 mL) was slowly added into 15 mL solution of the ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate (0.4 mmol) and thiourea (12 mmol)/100 mL aqueous solution under continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min at room temperature. The homogeneous solution was kept at 200 °C for 24 h. MoS2@CQDs catalysts were obtained after the products were dried in a vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h after washing several times. The catalyst powder of 5 mg was dispersed in a mixture of acetylene black (1 mg), ethanol (1 mL) and Nafion solution (50 μL, 5 wt%) to form the catalyst ink. The MoS2@CQDs ink of 5 μL was drop-cast onto the glassy carbon electrode (3 mm diameter) to fabricate MoS2@CQDs decorated glassy-carbon electrode. The catalyst ink of 20 μL was drop-cast onto the cleaned carbon paper to assess morphology and structure before and after chronoamperometry measurement.

The MoS2@CQDs catalysts were characterized by the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV with Cu Kα radiation) and Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectra (FTIR, (ABB Bomem MB-100 spectrometer). The morphologies were observed by the field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Inspect F50) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The fine structures were detected by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100) with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS ULTRA with Al-Kα X-rays) was used to monitor the surface charge states of the catalysts. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded by the N2 adsorption instrument (BSD-PS, China).

The HER performance of catalysts was conducted in 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution on the CHI760E electrochemical workstation. The MoS2@CQDs decorated glassy-carbon electrode, graphite rod electrode and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were acted as the working, counter and reference electrodes in a three-electrode cell, respectively. All the potentials were calibrated to a reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE) according to the equation
E
R H E
=
E
S C E
+ 0.059 × p H + 0.241
. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) profiles were employed to investigate overpotential of various electrodes with a scan rate of 2 mV/s from 0 to −0.4 V (vs. RHE). Tafel plots were converted from the corresponding LSV curves. The catalytic stability was recorded by chronoamperometry technique for 15 h. Quasi in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained on the GAMRY Reference 3000 electrochemistry workstation in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz of 5 mV alternating current voltage at various HER overpotentials. The double layer capacitance (Cdl) was determined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the non-faradaic region. The specific capacitance was generally found to be 0.04 mF/cm2 [26]. The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) can be calculated by following equation [27,28]:
(3)
E C S A =
C
d l
o f
c a t a l y s t
0.04
m F / c
m 2
c
m
E C S A
2

The electric quantity was calculated by the equation:
(4)
Q
t o t a l − C Q D s
=
Q
t o t a l
−
Q
M o
S 2
where Qtotal is the integration of the obtained C φ
vs. ƞ profiled for as-prepared catalysts. The C φ and ƞ are the adsorption pseudo-capacitance and overpotential.
Q
M o
S 2
is H* adsorption charge on MoS2 catalyst as reference. Qtotal-CQDs is the adsorption charge on CQDs surface.

The stability of interface coupling was evaluated by the adhesion energy (Eadh):
E
a d h
=
E T
−
E
M o
S 2
−
E
C Q D s
, where ET,
E
M o
S 2
and ECQDs were the energies of MoS2@CQDs, MoS2 and CQDs models. Eadh between MoS2 and CQDs was about −3.89 eV, demonstrating an exothermic process and thermodynamic stability. The AIMD simulation was used to further investigate the structural stability of the catalysts at 300 K for 10 ps (Movie S1). The oscillations of energy and temperature remained stable during the simulation process (Fig. S2), which could explain the dynamic structure stability of MoS2@CQDs. The distances of C–O, Mo–O and Mo–C atoms oscillated in a range of 1.42, 2.23 and 3.22 Å, respectively (Fig. S3). The results confirmed the thermodynamically and kinetically stable state of the MoS2@CQDs structure. Therefore, the enhanced HER performance of MoS2@CQDs can be predicted by the surface charge redistribution, electronic structure reformation and structure stability.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.07.071.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video S2 Video S2

The HER in alkaline involved two reaction steps of the adsorption and desorption [29]. The ΔGH* was calculated to assess reaction process at different sites of MoS2@MQDs catalysts (Fig. S4), where the adsorption sites were chosen based on the decreasing trend of charge distribution such as Site-a, Site-b, Site-c on CQDs and Site-d on MoS2. The ideal HER catalysts are expected as 0 eV of ΔGH* to balance the H* adsorption and subsequent H2 generation. The ΔGH* of MoS2 was optimized by the introduction of CQDs to enhance HER efficiency in a three-state diagram and the volcano plots (Fig. S5). The weak H* adsorption appeared on the site of the sulfur atom on MoS2 surface (Site-d, Fig. 3 ). The positive value of ΔGH* (1.53 eV) indicated that MoS2 was favorable for the desorption of H*, but not for the adsorption of H*. In contrast, H* preferentially adsorbed on the carbon atoms of CQDs with negative ΔGH* values of −1.35 eV (Site-a), −0.96 eV (Site-b) and −0.55 eV (Site-c). Therefore, the pathway of hydrogen spillover can be considered as the result of the superposition of the hydrogen evolution effects of MoS2 and CQDs. These specific processes can be regards as the hydrogen spillover pathways from Site-a to Site-d, mainly due to the changes of the charge density distributions and ΔGH* values (Fig. 3a). First, H* can be desorbed at Site-a, then sequentially through Site-b and Site-c, and finally desorb from Site-d. The whole process can be viewed as the hydrogen spillover pathway. Ultimately, the construction of hydrogen transport channels can be attributed to changes in electronic structure. The reorganization of electronic structure can lead to different interactions between adsorption sites and H*.

The COHP curves would explain the interaction of the adsorption sites and H* (Fig. 3b) [30]. We have drawn the bonding contributions to the right and the antibonding contributions to the left. All valence states (below EF) appeared as bonding for Site-a, Site-b and Site-c, while antibonding orbital populations were only detected in the conduction bands (above EF). This indicated the strong hydrogen adsorption on Site-a, Site-b and Site-c. Meanwhile, the weak hydrogen adsorption (positive ΔGH*) on Site-d occurred with increasing antibonding orbital population. The filling valence bands of the antibonding orbital populations were in accordance with the binding strength. The trend of hydrogen adsorption capacity was as following: Site-a > Site-b > Site-c > Site-d, demonstrating a possible hydrogen transport channel from Site-a to Site-d. Moreover, there was a linear correlation between -ICOHP and the adsorption energy of hydrogen adatom (ΔEH*, Fig. 3c). This linear correlation quantitatively explained the different adsorption sites of MoS2@CQDs and thus determined the roles in the bonding/antibonding orbital populations. Therefore, the theoretical hydrogen spillover pathway was able to predict kinetics of the catalyst during the HER process.

The CQDs were prepared by electro-oxidation method, separated by controlling the centrifugal rate to gain the different diameters of CQDs, and then loaded on MoS2 surface by hydrothermal method (Fig. 4 a). The average diameters were about 2.37 ± 0.96, 1.99 ± 0.66, and 1.03 ± 0.49 nm (Fig. S6), corresponding to LCQDs, MCQDs and SCQDs at the various centrifugal rate of 3000 r/min, 6000 r/min, and 10000 r/min, respectively. The schematic diagram of hydrothermal load CQDs was shown in Fig. S7a to product MoS2@LCQDs, MoS2@MCQDs, and MoS2@SCQDs catalysts. All diffraction peaks were consistent with the (002), (100), (103), (110) and (201) planes of MoS2 hexagonal phase (JCPDS No. 37–1492) (Fig. S7b). The morphologies evolution of MoS2@XCQDs (X = L, M, S) had hardly changed (Figs. S7c and S8). MoS2@MCQDs was further characterized by HRTEM (Fig. 4b, S9 and S10). The lattice fringe of 0.64 nm conformed to an interplanar distance of MoS2 (002), corresponding to SAED pattern (Fig. 4c). The interplanar spacings of 0.25 and 0.32 nm corresponded to the (101) and (002) facets of CQDs using the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (Fig. 4d). MCQDs can be clearly observed in high magnification TEM (Fig. 4e and f), corresponding to the elemental mapping and content of Mo, S, C, and O (Fig. 4g, S11). The C, O and Mo elements were uniformly distributed over the entire surface, possibly forming C–O–Mo bonds. The peaks at 1122 and 690 cm−1 appearing in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S12a) corresponded to S=O and M−O bonds in accordance with EDS results (Fig. S11). The chemical states of MoS2@MCQDs surface were detected by the XPS (Fig. S12b). The spectra of Mo 3d could be divided into six peaks with the bonding energy at approximate 226.2 eV of S 2s (Fig. S13a). The peaks of 229.0 and 231.9 eV were characteristic of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of Mo4+. Two peaks around 233.2 and 236.1 eV were fitted into the Mo6+ 3d orbit due to the appearance of MoO4 2+. The existence of Mo5+ (∼229.7 eV) can be attributed to the incomplete sulfide intermediate phase. As for S 2p, 2p3/2 shifted to low bonding energy, indicating O doping or S vacancies (Fig. S13b). Three characteristic peaks were located at 284.7, 286.6 and 288.5 eV, which was assigned to C–C, C–O and O=C–O bonding for C 1s (Fig. S13c). The O–Mo bond occurred at ∼532.1 eV in O 1s spectra (Fig. S13d), which was a typical metal-oxygen bond. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded to determine the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curves of MoS2 and MoS2@MCQDs (Fig. S14) exhibited a type IV adsorption branch with the H3 hysteresis loop, demonstrating the existence of a mesoporous structure. The MoS2@MCQDs BET surface area of 42.55 m2/g and total pore volume of 0.23 cm3/g) were higher than those of MoS2 (10.15 m2/g and 0.19 cm3/g). The higher BET surface area of MoS2@MCQDs can be attributed to the decreased size of MoS2 nanosheets. The high BET surface area of MoS2@MCQDs can ensure a huge number of activity sites to improve HER performances. These characterized results accorded to theoretical predictions and determined the applicability of the theoretical models.

The electrocatalytic activity was investigated using a three-electrode cell with a loading of 0.295 mg/cm2 on the working electrode, including the pristine MoS2, MoS2@LCQDs, MoS2@MCQDs, MoS2@SCQDs and the commercial Pt/C. Fig. 5 a shows the LSV curves to compare the overpotentials. The overpotential of MoS2@MCQDs was reduced by 71 mV under a current density of 10 mA/cm2 (Table S1) in contrast to MoS2. MoS2@MCQDs achieved a high current density (35 mA/cm2) at an overpotential of 300 mV, which confirmed that the coupling of CQDs and MoS2 could enhance the alkaline HER activity. The Tafel slope was calculated as 101 mV/dec for MoS2@MCQDs less than that of pristine MoS2 (132 mV/dec) (Fig. 5b). The Tafel slope of pristine MoS2 exceeded 120 mV/dec, suggesting that water dissociation was the rate-determining step for bare MoS2 in the alkaline solution. The decreased Tafel slopes of MoS2@CQDs catalysts demonstrated that CQDs could be beneficial to accelerate water dissociation on the MoS2 surface. The ECSA values were used to further evaluate intrinsic activity (Fig. 5c). The values can be estimated by the measurement of the Cdl from CV curves (Figs. S15 and S16). The MoS2@MCQDs had the larger ECSA of 113 cm2 than others, indicating its excellent catalytic performance. The ECSA values of MoS2@LCQDs, MoS2@MCQDs and MoS2@SCQDs were 1.94, 2.38 and 1.24 times higher than that of the pristine MoS2, respectively. The quasi in-situ EIS measurement can provide insights to the reaction kinetics during HER process (Fig. S17) [31]. The Nyquist plots were simulated by a double-parallel equivalent circuit model. The Rs and R1 represented the solution resistance and the charge-transfer resistance. The similar Rs values and the small R1 values for MoS2@LCQDs suggested the good conductivity and fast charge-transfer kinetics during HER process (Table S2). The R2 and C φ were the proton-transfer resistance and pseudo-capacitance, which was important parameters to describe the hydrogen adsorption behavior. Fig. 5d shows the derived curves of the adsorption pseudo-capacitance C φ
vs. ƞ. Their integration profiles demonstrated the H* adsorption charge on unit area of CQDs during HER process (Table S3), referring to Qtotal-CQDs. The MoS2@MCQDs gained a higher H* adsorption charge of 130.6 μC on CQDs (Qtotal-CQDs) than those of MoS2@LCQDs (64.2 μC) and MoS2@SCQDs (17.8 μC). The CQDs could significantly improve the local H* concentration on the surface of catalysts. The enhanced hydrogen collection on MoS2@CQDs improved the HER kinetics, agreeing with the DFT predictions.

MoS2@MCQDs maintained the initial current density after 15 h durability (Fig. 5e), in agreement with the AIMD simulations. After 15 h durability, the morphology and structure were also analyzed by the SEM (Figs. S18a and S18b), XRD (Fig. S18c), FTIR (Fig. S18d) and XPS (Fig. S19) characterizations. All measurements indicated the maintained morphology and structure of MoS2@MCQDs catalysts after 15 h durability. The HER performance of MoS2@MCQDs was comparable to the reported HER catalysts (Tables S4 and S5). The above results supported that MoS2@MCQDs can be an efficient and stable catalysts for alkaline HER.

In summary, we reported the utilization of CQDs as electron donors to facilitate the redistribution of surface charges on MoS2, achieve a gradient distribution for proton adsorption, and accelerate the transfer of protons from the surface of CQDs to the surface of MoS2 to enhance the hydrogen evolution rate. The multicomponent and multidimensional MoS2@CQDs fully integrated the regulation of electronic structure, adsorption and desorption equilibrium, and size effect to provide an important reference for the design and preparation of efficient hydrogen spillover pathway.

Lili Song: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyun Zhang: Formal analysis. Shifan Zhu: Investigation. Yixue Xu: Writing – original draft. Yuqiao Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that influence the work reported in this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61774033). We thank the Big Data Center of Southeast University for providing the facility support on the numerical calculations in this paper.
